discussion_comments.comment_body:
This behavior is due to implementation differences in how libc handles nan(n-char-sequence).

The proposed fix is insufficient. A mismatch between eptr and pend means the parsing was incomplete, so even if nan is detected, the input shouldn’t be accepted.

A more correct approach would be to relax the restriction in fast_float_strtod.c (https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/unstable/src/fast_float_strtod.c#L99) and allow any character sequence within nan(...), rather than limiting it to [a-zA-Z_-].

Even so, I’m inclined to preserve the existing behavior. There are likely other differences across libc implementations, and at some point we have to depend on the semantics.

Should we adjust the test instead and move these libc-dependent `nan(...)` cases into a separate helper that verifies `fast_float_strtod()` matches the platform `strtod()` behavior, including value, `endptr`, and success/failure status?

safe to assume we are on the default rounding. 
default for gcc and clang is to use -fno-rounding-math which meaning round to nearest.

it's not engout for this overflow check.
e.g. emission_interval_us = 3, num_requests = LONG_MAX, now increment_us > 3
Actually, I have been considering whether we should introduce the __builtin_*_overflow method, which can simply verify whether there is an overflow.

Hmm correct.
About the `builtin_*_overflow` - I see support starts with gcc5, but we support gcc 4.8..

Fixed with [06944e9](https://github.com/redis/redis/pull/14826/commits/06944e9ec487883766620214901485fd86d065a1). Initial checks were checking for addition overflow, whereas we should have checked for multiplication overflow. 

Will add generic overflow functions for all arithmetic operations in a future PR.
generally sparse has a few elements, maybe linear search is better

Should probably use linear under a certain size binsearch over a given size indeed. But for now given that this is going to be RC soon, I would skip all the kind of optimizations. It's very fast already. We can do it immediately after RC.
I'm not sure if I understand correctly. If lp changes at `lpReplaceInteger(lp,&p,marked_deleted+1)`, even if streamListpackCompaction() does not reallocate lp, should still need raxInsert()?

This seems very fragile and inaccurate to me.
A context switch between the system call and the he clock reading, would cause inaccuracies that in rare cases could be huge, and besides a small inaccuracy can cause a huge drift after a long period. 
Luckily we don't use the monotonic clock for anything important, but still, I think this manual calibration is wrong.
How about using it only when the parsing of the official one fails?
